findings.description: get() calls load() on every invocation, which re-opens and re-parses the entire YAML config file from disk (and re-scans os.environ) each time; require() and feature_enabled() route through get() too, so any call site reading N values triggers N synchronous disk reads + N full YAML parses with zero caching despite file/env being immutable for the process lifetime — blocking I/O on the hot path if any accessor is called per-request.

feature_enabled() triggers a full load() (file read + YAML parse + environ scan) just to read one nested flag; feature-flag checks are typically frequent/per-request, making this the most likely hot path for the no-caching root cause — a single cached/memoized merged config eliminates the repeated work.

_read_env() iterates all of os.environ with a per-key startswith on every load() call even though the environment is fixed at process start; redundant work compounded by the per-call load() pattern, fully subsumed by caching the merged config.
yaml.load(f) without SafeLoader permits arbitrary Python object deserialization (RCE) — cross-reference only; severity driver is security, defer detail to critic-security.

load() applies _read_env() before _read_file(), so file values override env vars — the inverse of the documented 'environment > config file' precedence (lines 2-3); correctness issue, defer detail to critic-logic.
